title: The Devil is Dead, by R.A. Lafferty - Book Review
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=onlJoxwtQec

October's first review is an honest wrestle with a book that won. Daniel (@dungeondive) takes on R.A. Lafferty's The Devil is Dead — sold by Avon as a science-fiction quest, actually something closer to magical realism he'd shelve beside Borges, Calvino, or Juan Rulfo's Pedro Páramo. The premise, loosely: a crew of seamen sail port to port through surreal adventures, trailed by doubles and body-doubles, with Papa Diabolus aboard — who may be the devil, or the devil's double. Daniel's verdict is unusually candid: the prose is exquisite (no one in the field matched Lafferty as a stylist), individual vignettes sparkle, and yet the novel never took hold of him — 'a purely intellectual exercise' he stopped thinking about the moment he set it down, and by his own admission possibly a book above his head. The consolation prizes are excellent: a strong recommendation for Nine Hundred Grandmothers, Lafferty's incredible story collection, a theory that Lafferty's obscurity comes from being unshelvable (with a Dhalgren comparison for company), and a glorious tangent contrasting Leo and Diane Dillon's gorgeous original covers with the 'raging garbage' 80s reprints — plus a threatened future video on the decline of genre cover art.

----

Daniel respected this book without liking it — exquisite prose, no pull. Can you love a writer's sentences while the novel leaves you cold, or is that a failed book by definition?
